title: Anger at the Coach Who Sold You a Story
dek: You trusted a coach to guide you through your inner landscape, but instead, they sold you a story that left your body tight and your heart bruised. This isn’t about failure or weakness; it’s about the very real, pulsing anger that arises when your body and intuition are sidelined. Here, you will learn to sit with that anger, honor the muscles that remember, and reclaim your narrative with somatic wisdom and herbal allies.
bodyMarkdown:
## That Moment the Story Shatters


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.974Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You’re sitting at your kitchen table, the late afternoon light slanting in through the blinds, dust motes dancing in the fading warmth. Maybe you’re alone, or maybe the house hums quietly with life around you. Your phone buzzes - a message from the coach who promised you transformation, who sold you a story of empowerment and breakthrough. But today, the words land like a fist to the solar plexus. The story unravels, thread by thread, revealing a narrative that never matched the truth you lived.

The betrayal isn’t just intellectual; it isn’t a simple matter of being lied to. Your body knows. The moment you recognize the gap between the promise and the reality, your chest tightens, the breath catches behind your sternum. You feel the familiar flare-up, the ignition of something fierce and ancient. This is not just anger; this is your body screaming, “I was fooled. I was sold a lie.” The masseter muscle clenches involuntarily; your jaw locks so tight it’s as if you’re bracing against a blow, the teeth pressing into the gums with a dull ache that radiates down to the temporomandibular joint.

You remember sitting in that first session, the coach’s voice smooth and confident, their eyes steady like a harbor light. You wanted to believe. You had to believe. But now the facade crumbles, and the raw, ragged edges of your fury pulse through the levator scapulae, that tight ribbon of muscle running up the neck, the familiar tension that signals your body’s readiness to defend, to fight, to resist.

## The Body Remembers the Betrayal

Resmaa Menakem teaches us that trauma, anger, and rage are not just in the mind; they live in the body’s fascia, the connective tissue that wraps and holds every muscle and organ in a vast, intricate web. When you feel that betrayal, it isn’t just a story in your head - it’s a somatic event, a ripple in your nervous system. The trapezius muscles, those broad wings spanning your upper back, tighten reflexively, pulling your shoulders up as if to shield your heart from further harm.

Your diaphragm, the dome-shaped muscle beneath your ribs that orchestrates your breath, flattens and constricts. Breathing becomes shallow, clipped, the body’s urgent response to a perceived threat. You might notice your fingers twitching or clenching, the tendons taut beneath the skin. These are not random reactions; they are your body’s language of anger, a primal script written in muscle and fascia, in sweat and breath.

Menakem’s work reminds you that anger is not the enemy. It’s the body’s messenger, a signal that something inside you has been violated - your trust, your dignity, your sense of safety. This is not pathology. This is biology, hardwired and immediate. Your body is responding to the coach’s betrayal as if it were a physical wound, because in a very real way, it is.

## The Slow Burn of Realization

The first flash of anger might feel like a quick flare - heat rising to your ears, a sharp clench in your belly. But as the minutes stretch into hours, you notice the slow burn settling in: the persistent ache along your spine, the dull throb behind your eyes, the restless agitation in your calves. Your nervous system is stuck in a loop, replaying the moment you saw through the story, the moment the foundation cracked beneath your feet.

This is the betrayal that no pep talk can erase, no motivational quote can soothe. It’s the feeling that you were not just misled but used, your vulnerability a commodity sold back to you in glossy packaging. Your body protests, the psoas muscle - deep in your abdomen, the seat of fight-or-flight - tightening like a coiled spring, ready to launch. You might feel a bitter taste at the back of your throat, the metallic tang of raw, unprocessed rage.

## Trusting the Body’s Wisdom

The teachings of Resmaa Menakem guide you to sit with this anger, to locate it within your tissues without flinching or shame. The body remembers the betrayal long before the mind can name it. Your task is not to silence this fury but to listen. The trapezius and levator scapulae hold the story of your defense; your diaphragm’s constriction tells you where the breath has been stolen. These are clues, not curses.

You might find yourself pacing the floor late at night, muscles screaming beneath your skin, the scent of your own sweat sharp in the stale air. This is the body speaking its truth. Herbal allies like skullcap or passionflower could offer a gentle balm for the nervous system’s overload, but only if you honor the anger first. To rush past it is to ignore the body’s wisdom, to deny the legitimacy of your response.

In this moment, you are not broken. You are a living archive of experience, your muscles and fascia holding the memory of the coach’s betrayal with unflinching honesty. Your anger is not ugly or wrong; it is the body’s declaration that you deserve better. The story they sold you was never yours to carry. Your body knows this, and it will not let you forget.

## When Anger Shows Up to Draw the Line

You’re sitting in the half-light of your living room, the late afternoon sun slanting low and warm on your forearm. You can feel it: a tightness coiling just beneath your ribs, the subtle flare of your nostrils, an almost imperceptible quiver in your lower jaw where the masseter muscle clenches itself awake. This is your anger. It’s not an ugly thing, even if you’ve been taught to think so. It is the body’s way of saying, “Stop. This is not okay.” Karla McLaren, a fierce and compassionate guide in the territory of emotions, calls anger the ultimate boundary-setter. She insists that anger is not a failure of character or a moral lapse but an essential, finely tuned signal from your nervous system designed to protect your integrity.

Think about the coach - the one who sold you a story. Maybe it was a mentor, a boss, a parent, or a partner who convinced you that your worth depended on meeting their expectations, that your needs were secondary, or that your voice was too loud, too much, too unseemly. They didn’t just hand you advice; they handed you a script, a narrative that clipped your edges and left you feeling smaller, less than whole. This is the moment your anger arrives, sharp and undeniable, like the sudden tightening of the trapezius muscle when you sense a threat. It’s your body’s way of slamming on the brakes, drawing a line in the fascia that surrounds your ribs and spine, saying, “No more.”

McLaren teaches that anger’s first job is to mark the boundary between what is yours and what is not. It’s a guardian of your personal space, a protector of your energetic and emotional ecosystem. When you suppress this anger, when you swallow it whole because you were told it’s ugly or dangerous, you invite invasion. The psoas muscle tightens, the diaphragm constricts, and your breath becomes shallow as your body tries to hold itself small, contained, invisible. But the body knows. The jaw remembers. And eventually, that anger will find its way out, often in ways that seem disproportionate or confusing because it was never given the chance to do its real work.

## Feeling the Edges of Your Anger

To work with your anger as Karla McLaren suggests, you need to become the cartographer of your own inner landscape. You need to trace the borders where your anger arises and see what it protects. This is not about intellectualizing or pushing your anger away but about allowing it to be felt fully, with curiosity and respect. The body provides the map if you’re willing to pay attention.

Start by noticing where the anger lives in your body. Is it a heat rising behind your eyes? A tightening in the levator scapulae, the muscle that runs along the side of your neck? Maybe it’s a churning in the solar plexus or the sudden alertness of the diaphragm as it presses upward, restricting your breath. Don’t try to change it or judge it; just be with it. Imagine the fascia - the dense, web-like connective tissue that glues your muscles and organs together - pulling taut, drawing a circle around the place where your body says, “This is mine.”

When you can identify this territory, you begin to understand what your anger is protecting: your time, your energy, your truths. It’s not a wild beast to be tamed but a messenger demanding you listen. McLaren’s work helps you translate this visceral language into clear boundaries that you can state and hold in the world, not as threats but as invitations to respect your wholeness.

## An Embodied Practice: Listening to Anger’s Boundary

This practice is for the late evening, when the world is quieter and your body can feel the contrast between tension and release more clearly. The room is dim, maybe scented faintly with the sharp, cleansing aroma of fresh peppermint oil on your wrists. The air is cool, just below 70 degrees, carrying the promise of night’s rest.

Sit on a firm chair or on the floor with a cushion beneath your sit bones. Your feet root into the ground, the soles heavy and solid. Close your eyes softly and bring your attention inward. Begin with three slow, deliberate breaths, feeling the diaphragm expand and contract like the tide. Inhale, the belly rises; exhale, it falls. Notice the subtle tension that signals your anger. You might feel it as a knot in the psoas or a gripping of the masseter muscle in your jaw. Don’t force or resist. Instead, lean into this sensation gently, as if acknowledging a wary animal at the edge of your garden.

As you breathe, silently ask your anger, “What are you protecting right now?” Don’t expect words. Instead, notice the shifting sensations: a tightening here, a warmth there, a subtle pulse like a heartbeat beneath the skin. Your anger is telling you where your boundary lies. Let the body’s intelligence unfold this message without hurry.

Now, with your hand, trace the outline of this boundary on your own body. If your anger feels like it lives in your chest and ribs, place your palms there, feeling the rise and fall of your breath beneath your hands. If it’s in your neck or jaw, gently massage the levator scapulae or the masseter muscle, acknowledging their work in holding this boundary. This is not to soothe the anger away but to honor it, to show it you see its vigilance.

When you feel ready, open your eyes and write a sentence or two in a journal that begins with, “My anger tells me I need…” or “This boundary is about…” Let this be a statement of your truth, a clear line in the sand drawn with the ink of your own body’s knowing.

## Carrying the Boundary into the Day

The next time the coach’s story tries to seduce you into silence or self-doubt, remember the territory you traced in your practice. Remember that your anger is not a flaw but the sacred border patrol of your inner world. It’s there to protect you from erosion, from being worn down into a shape that doesn’t fit your bones.

Karla McLaren’s teachings remind you that anger is a language, one that your body speaks fluently and urgently. When you learn to listen - not with the ears but with the soft tissues and the breath - you reclaim your power. You stop being a passive recipient of someone else’s story and start authoring your own, grounded in the fierce, unyielding wisdom of your body’s boundaries. This is not just survival; it is the deep, embodied flourishing that comes when you honor your anger as the guardian it is.

## Herbal Allies When the Body Holds the Betrayal

You wake in the early hours, the room still thick with the scent of rain, the cold pressing into your trapezius muscles like a slow knife. It’s here, in the liminal space between sleep and wake, that the story the coach sold you coils tight in your diaphragm. The breath, trapped behind a barricade of unmet promises, tastes faintly of iron and old woodsmoke. This is not a failure of will or character. This is your body, speaking through the language of constricted fascia and clenched masseter muscles, showing you that the betrayal is lodged deep, a bruise beneath the skin of your confidence.

Bessel van der Kolk’s work on trauma as a bodily imprint reminds us that the mind’s narrative is only part of the story. Trauma - whether from a coach who sold you a false narrative or a system that failed to protect you - registers first and most potently in the body. The nervous system stays alert, the amygdala flickers with false alarms, and the muscles remember. They remember the tightening in your levator scapulae, the sudden surge in your psoas when you think of that person’s smiling face that never quite matched their words.

Herbs enter this somatic conversation as skilled translators. They do not promise to erase the story, but they can soothe the raw edges, soften the grip of unrelenting tension, and help the nervous system find its way back to calm. Take *skullcap* ([Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20)), a venerable nervine that eases the firing of overloaded neurons. The way skullcap mingles with your bloodstream feels like a cool mist settling over the back of your neck, coaxing the trapezius to unclench and the diaphragm to drop, allowing a breath that had been held hostage to finally come home.

Similarly, *passionflower* ([Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20)) carries the bittersweet wisdom of the twilight hours. It works gently on GABA receptors, the brain’s natural brakes, helping to dial down the hypervigilance that Bessel van der Kolk describes so vividly in *The Body Keeps the Score*. When you sip a warm infusion just before bed, the levator scapulae relaxes, and the persistent clench in your jaw - the masseter muscle’s silent scream - loosens enough for sleep to visit without the usual resistance.

In the somatic teachings of Resmaa Menakem, we learn that trauma is stored not just in the mind but in the body’s muscle memory, in the quiet tension of the connective tissue. Herbs like *ashwagandha* ([Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20)) come into play here as adaptogens, modulating the stress response and fortifying the parasympathetic nervous system. Ashwagandha feels like a grounding force, a subtle but persistent weight in the belly that tells you, This is safe now. It helps to retrain the psoas muscle, often held in a chronic fight-or-flight squeeze, to release its clenched grip on the lower spine.

But the medicine does not stop at herbs alone. The body remembers, as Karla McLaren teaches, and it must be allowed to express. The herbs serve as allies, not anesthetics. You draw the line with gentleness and resoluteness, tending the wound with a blend of bitter tinctures and the raw honesty of your own breath. You might find yourself chanting a mantra from Pema Chödrön or following a somatic movement sequence to wake the fascia after hours of rigidity. The herbs support this dance, the movement a slow unspooling of the story that was sold to you and the fierce reclaiming of your own narrative.

## The Body Remembers: Bessel van der Kolk’s Wisdom in Practice

Bessel van der Kolk’s insight that trauma is “stuck” in the body reframes the rage you feel - not as a flaw or failure, but as a biological echo of harm. The coach’s story wasn’t just words; it was an energetic imprint that lodged itself in your nervous system, a trauma that the brain and body have been trying to process ever since. This means your anger is not random or unearned; it is the body’s urgent signal that boundaries were crossed, trust was broken, and the nervous system needs repair.

Van der Kolk emphasizes that healing requires reconnecting with the body, not abstracting the experience into mere thought or emotion. This is why somatic approaches - those that honor the musculature, the breath, the subtle shifts in blood flow - are essential. The herbs you take become part of this reconnection, their molecules weaving through your bloodstream, calming the hyperactive limbic system, and inviting the body to soften its defense.

When you take a tincture of *valerian* ([FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20)) in the late evening, you may notice the subtle loosening in your chest, the way your diaphragm, usually rigid and constricted, begins to expand and contract with less resistance. This is not a magic erasure of trauma but a gentle invitation to your body to remember what it feels like to be at ease, even if only for a moment.

Van der Kolk’s teachings remind you to respect the body’s pace. Healing is not linear, and the anger that flares might return as new memories surface or as your nervous system tests the safety of your current environment. The herbs, combined with somatic awareness, provide a steady anchor through these storms.

## FAQ: Navigating Anger and Herbal Support After Betrayal

**Q: Can herbs really help with anger caused by betrayal, or is this just emotional work?**  
**A:** Your body and mind are inseparable. Anger, especially when tied to deep wounds like betrayal, lives in your nervous system and muscular structure. Herbs like skullcap and passionflower soothe the nervous system’s overdrive, making it easier for you to feel and express anger without being overwhelmed by it. This supports the emotional work by giving your body the space to release tension safely.

**Q: How do I know which herb to use when my body feels tense from this kind of anger?**  
**A:** Notice where you hold the tension. If your neck and shoulders are tight - your trapezius and levator scapulae - skullcap might be a good fit. If your jaw is clenched and sleep is elusive, passionflower can help. For chronic stress held in the abdomen and psoas, ashwagandha provides grounding. The key is listening to your body’s subtle signals and, if possible, consulting with an herbalist who understands trauma.

**Q: Is it safe to combine these herbs with other medications?**  
**A:** Herb-drug interactions can happen. If you are on medications like antidepressants or blood pressure drugs, check with your healthcare provider before adding herbs like valerian or ashwagandha. Herbs are powerful medicines and deserve the same caution as pharmaceuticals.

**Q: Can I use herbs to numb or suppress my anger?**  
**A:** Herbs are not anesthetics for emotional pain. They support your nervous system’s ability to process and express emotions healthily. Suppressing anger usually causes it to ferment in the body, increasing tension and prolonging suffering. The goal is to feel your anger fully, with the body’s support, not to silence it.

**Q: How long does it take for these herbal remedies to help with trauma-related anger?**  
**A:** Trauma healing is not a quick fix. Herbs can start calming your nervous system within days, but rewiring the body’s response to betrayal takes weeks or months. Consistency and patience, combined with somatic practices and, when needed, professional support, create the conditions for sustainable change.

Your anger at the coach who sold you a story is a fierce, embodied truth. It demands to be heard, held, and honored. With the wisdom of teachers like Bessel van der Kolk and the medicine of herbs, you can begin to untangle the story from your body’s biology, releasing the tension that no longer serves you and reclaiming your power one breath, one muscle, one moment at a time.

---
